Patch Set 15: Code-Review-1

I fear that this approach is fundamentally flawed.  If the goal is to "Make readonly patches optional", then why is the osd_ldiskfs_debug.ko now built _unconditionally_ when ldiskfs is built?  That is a regression in functionality as far as I can tell.  That means that we are now _required_ to have a patched kernel in order to build ldiskfs.

Previously, the ldiskfs at least used "#ifdef HAVE_DEV_SET_RDONLY" to try to detect whether the kernel was patched at configure time, and compile ldiskfs to suit.  Does this patch not break all that?  Am I missing something?

Furthermore, it makes the kmp-lustre-tests impossible to install with an unpatched kernel, because it will now contain osd_ldiskfs_debug.ko which requires a patched kernel.

Keep in mind that we need to be able lustre _with_ ldiskfs, but without a patched kernel.  Please, please, please, don't break that.

Hmmm, I see.

Well, yes, I would still fully expect this to prevent the install of the rpm.  The rpm "requires" section contains a full list of all required kernel symbols (and their symbol version).  So I would not expect the test rpm to be installable on a system without a patched kernel installed.

Unless of course this _also_ breaks the symbol dependency system.  And that would be reason not to land this as well.

Patch Set 15:

It looks to me like this approach winds up breaking symbol versioning for the forward declared functions.  That means that standard toosl like "modprobe --dump-modversions" won't know anything about those required symbols, so rpm can't know anything about them.  So maybe the test rpm could be installed...but then depending on the version of weak-modules that is installed, the module might be installed under _all_ kernels, even the ones that don't have the require symbols.

That is quite a bit more messy than I would like to see.

Patch Set 25:

OK, now you are just going to hate me. :)

Honestly, I'm not a fan of this approach.  I think that we really need to keep most autoconf configure-time changes out of the decision making process in generating spec file details.

The only changes that can be made by autoconf to the spec files are those that fit into the realm of the "configure --enable-dist", which bypass the more extensive system tests of "configure".  This is almost certainly going beyond that rule.

Basically, you are introducing a bit of a chicken-and-egg problem.  We should be able to do a "make dist", and then the resulting tarball can be used anywhere.  But that tar ball, with this patch, will already have some decisions made in the spec file.  We can't necessarily just do a straight "rpmbuild" using the tar ball and provided spec file.  We may need to go through a whole round of "make configure" only to make the spec files, and then go through _another_ whole round of "make configure".

You're making it so we _have_ to run the full configure process twice.  We want to avoid that.

Patch Set 25: -Code-Review

Currently the creation of a separate osd_ldiskfs_debug.ko is from earlier versions of this patch where Oleg wanted to have the "debug" module always available. This way if the kernel went from patch-less to a patched kernel version osd-ldiskfs was magically worked with the test suite instead of failing. What happened instead is that you got installation issues due to osd_ldiskfs_debug.ko referencing none existent functions in the kernel when it was patch-less. The easiest solution is just build osd_ro_handler.o into osd_ldiskfs.ko when a patched kernel is detected. Then we only deal with one module. As it stands now with the patch if you move from a patched kernel to an patch-less kernel the test sutie will fail. Oleg are you okay with the have just one module for osd-ldiskfs instead of two.

Patch Set 25:

We cannot have one module

The whole idea is this:
you have a osd_ldiskfs.ko that works on both patched AND unpatched kernel with no changes (the weak updates magic).
Now that means we get no readonly support of course because we cannot depend on it.
So the _debug counterpart registers the hooks like that and depends on the symbols (And fails to load if the kernel is unpatched).
Now Chris does not want the module installed because versioning is broken and whatnot.

Originally I wanted to always build release packages against patched kernels so it did not matter, and who cares what happens on unpatched kernels anyway (if it builds).

We already have a bit of autoconf logic in specs, but if there's a better way to optionally include a file - I am all ears!
This was the most easy way I found so far.

commit.subject: LU-20 osd-ldiskfs: Make readonly patches optional
commit.message:
LU-20 osd-ldiskfs: Make readonly patches optional

This patch creates additional module called osd_ldiskfs_debug
that would call symbols from read_only patches.
The idea is you would only insert it for debugging sessions
that require the functionality and using the patched kernel.

For production use and performance runs you would then skip this
module and weak_updates magic would allow normal osd_ldiskfs
module to be inserted and run normally (same for performance runs).

The new module would be rolled into lustre-tests rpm
